claims.claim_text: The opening incident vignette says a platform team joined an incident bridge at 02:13 during a holiday promotion.
claims.anchor_text: A platform team joined an incident bridge at 02:13 after a checkout API stopped accepting writes during a holiday promotion.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A platform team joined an incident bridge after a checkout API stopped accepting writes during a high-traffic event.
claims.rationale: The vignette uses fake-precise incident timing and event context without an attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The opening incident vignette says kubectl waited almost a minute for the API server to answer.
claims.anchor_text: Then the on-call engineer ran `kubectl get pods` and waited almost a minute for the API server to answer.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Then the on-call engineer ran `kubectl get pods` and saw the API server respond slowly.
claims.rationale: The precise response-time detail is part of an unattributed synthetic incident.

claims.claim_text: The vignette says the platform originally served two internal teams before becoming a production substrate.
claims.anchor_text: That decision made sense when the platform served two internal teams, but the cluster had quietly become the production substrate for payment processing, order routing, and customer support tools.
claims.suggested_rewrite: That decision can make sense while a platform serves a small number of internal teams, but the cluster can quietly become the production substrate for critical business systems.
claims.rationale: The exact team count and operational evolution are presented as factual details but are not sourced.

claims.claim_text: Amazon EKS is a managed Kubernetes service where AWS manages the Kubernetes control plane.
claims.rationale: The Amazon EKS overview directly describes EKS as managed Kubernetes and states that AWS manages the control plane.

claims.claim_text: Amazon EKS managed node groups automate provisioning and lifecycle management of EC2 worker nodes.
claims.span_hint: section: Amazon EKS; table row Common operating mode
claims.rationale: The EKS managed node groups page directly states that managed node groups automate node provisioning and lifecycle management.

claims.claim_text: EKS Fargate uses profiles to select Kubernetes pods that run on AWS Fargate.
claims.span_hint: EKS architecture diagram and comparison table
claims.rationale: The EKS Fargate profile documentation explains that selectors determine which pods run on Fargate.

claims.claim_text: Amazon EKS add-ons include CoreDNS, kube-proxy, Amazon VPC CNI, and Amazon EBS CSI Driver.
claims.rationale: The Amazon EKS add-ons page lists the named add-ons and their management model.

claims.claim_text: The Amazon VPC CNI assigns pod IP addresses from the AWS VPC.
claims.span_hint: EKS architecture diagram and comparison table
claims.rationale: The EKS VPC CNI documentation directly states that the add-on assigns private IPv4 or IPv6 addresses from the VPC to pods.

claims.claim_text: IRSA maps AWS IAM roles to Kubernetes service accounts through OIDC and avoids distributing static AWS credentials to containers.
claims.rationale: The EKS IRSA page describes associating IAM roles with Kubernetes service accounts through OIDC instead of distributing AWS credentials.

claims.claim_text: GKE has Standard and Autopilot modes with different control and node-management responsibilities.
claims.rationale: The GKE mode-selection page explains Standard and Autopilot operation modes and their tradeoffs.

claims.claim_text: In GKE Autopilot, Google manages worker nodes and reduces node-level management for users.
claims.rationale: The Autopilot overview states that Google manages worker nodes and node lifecycle tasks.

claims.claim_text: GKE Autopilot uses pod resource requests for placement and billing-related resource management.
claims.span_hint: lines 501-503 and Did You Know item 2
claims.rationale: The Autopilot resource requests page explains how Autopilot uses and adjusts pod resource requests, including billing-model implications.

claims.claim_text: Workload Identity Federation for GKE lets Kubernetes workloads access Google Cloud APIs without service account key files and with fine-grained identities.
claims.rationale: The GKE Workload Identity Federation page directly describes keyless access and fine-grained identity for workloads.

claims.claim_text: GKE Autopilot blocks or restricts privileged and node-level workloads by default.
claims.rationale: The Autopilot security page documents restrictions on privileged containers, host namespaces, host networking, and node-level access.

claims.claim_text: GKE release channels govern Kubernetes version selection and upgrade behavior.
claims.span_hint: GKE creation command discussion and comparison table
claims.rationale: The GKE release channels documentation explains channel-based version and upgrade management.

claims.claim_text: AKS is a managed Kubernetes service where Azure creates and manages the control plane.
claims.rationale: The AKS overview states that Azure automatically creates and manages the AKS control plane.

claims.claim_text: AKS supports system and user node pools, with system pools intended for critical system pods and user pools for application workloads.
claims.rationale: The AKS system node pools page directly defines system and user node pool purposes.

claims.claim_text: AKS Workload Identity uses a Kubernetes service account, OIDC federation, and Microsoft Entra managed identities for workload access to Azure resources.
claims.rationale: The AKS Workload Identity overview directly describes service account token projection, OIDC federation, and managed identity integration.

claims.claim_text: AKS virtual nodes enable pods to run in Azure Container Instances and communicate with the AKS cluster.
claims.span_hint: AKS architecture diagram and comparison table
claims.rationale: The AKS virtual nodes page directly explains the Azure Container Instances integration.

claims.claim_text: AKS supports Windows Server node pools for Windows container workloads.
claims.rationale: The AKS Windows Server node pool documentation directly covers creating Windows Server node pools for containers.

claims.claim_text: Azure CNI provides pod networking integration with Azure virtual networks.
claims.span_hint: AKS command discussion and comparison table
claims.rationale: The AKS CNI overview describes CNI plugins, pod IP assignment, routing, and virtual network connectivity.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes NetworkPolicy controls pod traffic only when the cluster uses a network plugin that enforces NetworkPolicy.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes NetworkPolicy documentation directly states plugin enforcement requirements and allow-rule behavior.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes schedules pods based on resource requests and enforces resource limits through kubelet/container runtime behavior.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes resource management page directly describes scheduler use of requests and limit enforcement.

claims.claim_text: PodDisruptionBudgets limit voluntary disruptions and are relevant to node drains and upgrade planning.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes disruptions page documents PDB behavior, voluntary disruptions, drains, and upgrade-related disruption handling.

claims.claim_text: Admission webhooks can reject API requests, and webhook errors or timeouts with fail policy can block object creation.
claims.rationale: The dynamic admission control documentation explains validating webhooks, request rejection, and failurePolicy behavior.

claims.claim_text: Deprecated Kubernetes APIs and customer-owned extensions must be checked before minor-version upgrades.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes deprecated API migration guide is the primary source for API removals and migration requirements across releases.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes Secrets are stored in the API server's underlying data store, etcd, by default.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes Secrets page directly states where Secrets are stored and discusses default at-rest exposure.

claims.claim_text: Amazon EKS pricing includes per-cluster fees plus separate charges for worker-node and related AWS resources.
claims.rationale: The Amazon EKS pricing page directly describes per-cluster pricing and separate charges for worker-node resources and related AWS infrastructure.

claims.claim_text: GKE pricing includes cluster management fees, Autopilot-related pricing, and compute or ingress charges.
claims.rationale: The GKE pricing page directly covers cluster management fees, compute resources, operation modes, and ingress fees.

claims.claim_text: AKS pricing distinguishes free, Standard, and Premium control-plane tiers and charges for compute resources.
claims.rationale: The AKS pricing page directly describes control-plane tiers and compute pricing.

further_reading.title: What is Amazon EKS?
further_reading.why_relevant: Official overview of the EKS managed control plane and AWS integration model.

further_reading.title: About GKE Modes of Operation
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains the Standard versus Autopilot decision that the module centers on.

further_reading.title: What is Azure Kubernetes Service?
further_reading.why_relevant: Official AKS overview covering managed control plane, Azure integration, and common AKS features.

further_reading.title: Network Policies
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary Kubernetes reference for the service-boundary and network-isolation parts of the module.

further_reading.title: Disruptions and PodDisruptionBudgets
further_reading.why_relevant: Supports the upgrade-readiness and disruption-budget guidance used in the hands-on baseline.

notes: Quiz questions, exercise steps, command blocks, and purely pedagogical decision-model guidance were not listed as claims unless they contained factual vendor, security, pricing, or Kubernetes API behavior that needs inline support.
